content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

October 30, 1995

Mr. Clayton Roche
Deputy Attorney General
Department of Justice
455 Golden Gate Avenue, Suite 6200
San Francisco, California 94102-3658

Re: Your Request for General
Assistance
Our File No. G-95-249

Dear Mr. Roche:

You received a request for an opinion of the Attorney General
(your opinion No. 95-324) from the State Board of Equalization
regarding their duties under the Quentin L. Kopp Conflict-of-
Interest Act of 1990 (Kopp Act). You are now soliciting the views
of the Fair Political Practices Commission concerning the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act")¹, pertaining to the same questions.

QUESTIONS

The State Board of Equalization posed the following questions
in its request to the Attorney General's Office:

1. When a majority of the members of the State Board of
Equalization ("Board") are prohibited from participating in the
making of a governmental decision pursuant to Government Code
Section 15626, may a sufficient number of disqualified members be
brought back to establish a quorum through a process of selection
by lot as provided in 18 California Code of Regulations 7008(d)?

2. If so, what form should the process of selection by lot
take?

_______________

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections 18000-18995. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless
otherwise noted.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. G-95-249
Page 2


     3.  In addition to selection by lot, what are the "other
means of random selection" that may be used as provided in
18 CCR 7008(d)?

     4.  Must all disqualified members participate in the
selection by lot for the process to be valid?  Is the answer the
same for other means of random selection?  For other impartial and
equitable means of selection?

     5.  When is a member's participation "legally required"
within the meaning of Government Code Section 15626(i)(5)?
Is the answer the same for both the Board's statutory duties and
constitutional duties?

     6.  What options are available to the Board for making a
governmental decision when a majority of the members are
prohibited from participating in the decision pursuant to the Kopp
Act?  Is one acceptable option to postpone the decision until
twelve months elapse from the date of the contribution?  Would the
option of postponing the decision be acceptable for the duties of
the Board that must be performed annually?

                              ANALYSIS

     The following analysis is an overview of the Act, as it
pertains to the questions posed above.  Under Section 87101 and
Regulation 18701, which was adopted by the Commission to implement
and implement this section, participation is legally required only
if there is no alternative means of decisionmaking.  "Legally
required participation" allows an otherwise disqualified member to
participate or vote only in those limited cases where his or her
vote is necessary to obtain the quorum needed to take action.

     In the Hudson Opinion, the Commission stated:

          We note that previous cases applying a common
     law "rule of necessity" appear to allow all
     disqualified members of a council or board to
     vote.  While the common law rule of necessity is
     analogous to the concept of "legally required
     participation" contained in the Political Reform
     Act, we believe that the purposes of the Act are
     best served by a rule which minimizes participation
     in government decisions by officials with a
     conflict of interest.  (Emphasis added.)

               (In re Hudson (1978) 4 FPPC
               Ops. 13, p. 17, copy enclosed.)

     Therefore, the concept of legally required participation is
to be narrowly construed so that the impact of the resulting bias
is minimized.  Section 87101 and Regulation 18701 codify and limit

File No. G-95-249
Page 3


the common law "rule of necessity." Regulation 18701(a) states
that:

     (a) A public official is not legally required
to make or to participate in the making of a
governmental decision within the meaning of Govern-
ment Code Section 87101 unless there exists no
alternative source of decision consistent with the
purposes and terms of the statute authorizing the
decision. (Emphasis added.)

This approach reflects the policy of Section 87101 to give effect
to both the policy of enabling a public body to act and the policy
of minimizing the effect of bias in making the decision.

Regulation 18701(c)(2) provides that the regulation shall be
construed narrowly and shall:

     (2) Not be construed to allow a member of any
public agency, who is otherwise disqualified under
Government Code Section 87100, to vote if a quorum
can be convened of other members of the agency who
are not disqualified under Government Code Section
87100, whether or not such other members are actu-
ally present at the time of the disqualification.

Therefore, a disqualified member cannot participate or vote
if a quorum can be convened of other members, whether or not they
are actually present. The FPPC has consistently advised that a
disqualified member may not be selected to make up a quorum if a
quorum can be convened of other members who are not disqualified,
whether or not such other members are actually present at the time
of the disqualification. (Cohen Advice Letter, No. A-94-274, copy
enclosed.) Therefore, when the failure to form a quorum is due to
the physical absence of another member, the participation of a
disqualified official is not legally required.

The Commission has advised in Hudson that only that number of
financially interested persons may participate in a decision as
may be necessary to constitute a quorum. In Hudson, the
Commission concluded that choosing by "lot or other means of
random selection" is the best method for forming a quorum from
disqualified members. (Hudson, supra, at p. 18.)

This advice has also been codified in 18 CCR 7008(d) as
follows:

     In the event a board member's participation is legally
required for the action or decision to be made, the
board may bring back as many disqualified members as is
necessary to establish a quorum. The preferred means of
selecting which disqualified member should participate
is by lot. Other means of random selection or other

File No. G-95-249
Page 4


     impartial and equitable means of selection may also be
used.

     The preceding analysis has given a broad overview of the
"legally required participation" provisions of the Act. The
answers to the specific questions are addressed as follows:

     1.  Under the Act, when a majority of the members of the
Board have a conflict of interest, a sufficient number of
disqualified members may be brought back to establish a quorum.
We have advised under Regulation 18701 that "legally required
participation" allows for disqualified members to be convened to
form a quorum only if a quorum cannot be convened of other
members. The Hudson Opinion concluded that choosing by "lot or
other means of random selection" is the best method to form a
quorum of disqualified members.

     2 and 3.  Our advice follows the Hudson Opinion which
generally allows selection of disqualified members by lot or other
means of random selection. Our advice has not been more specific
as to what other means of random selection may be used, but this
would generally include drawing straws or names from a hat.
However, any means would be acceptable as long as it is random
selection.

     4.  All disqualified members must participate in the random
selection in order for "legally required participation" to apply.
In a situation where all the disqualified members do not
participate in the selection process, the selection would not be
considered truly random. This same answer would apply to other
means of random selection as well as any other impartial and
equitable means of selection.

     5.  Under Section 87101 and Regulation 18701(a) of the Act, a
member's participation is "legally required" when there exists no
alternate form of decision consistent with the purpose and terms
of the statute authorizing the decision. The provision in
Government Code Section 15626(i)(5), as implemented by 18 CCR
7008(d), is essentially the same.

     Question five also asks if this answer applies to both the
Board's statutory duties and constitutional duties. Article XIII
of the California Constitution creates the Board and prescribes
certain duties and responsibilities with respect to property
taxes. Section 19 of Article XIII states in part that "The Board
shall annually assess..." certain specified property. This
section imposes a constitutional duty which must be performed
annually. We have not been provided any information regarding the
Board's statutory duties.

     Under the Act, a Board member's "legally required
participation" is allowed in situations where "there exists no
alternative source of decision consistent with the statute
authorizing the decision." We do not have enough information

File No. G-95-249
Page 5


regarding the second part of question five and it is not within
our jurisdiction to determine if the answer is the same for both
the Board's statutory and constitutional duties.

6. The first part of this question has been answered under
the discussion of "legally required participation" in question
one. It is not within our jurisdiction to determine whether an
annual duty of the Board or any decision of the Board may be
postponed.

If a decision is required by law to be made within a specific
period of time, "legally required participation" would apply to
members with a conflict of interest. However, if the Board is
making a decision to postpone a matter, the Board is making a
governmental decision to which both the conflict-of-interest
provisions and the laws of "legally required participation" would
apply. If it is a staff function to remove or postpone a matter
on the calendar, the Board is not involved.

I trust this answers your questions. Please contact our
office if you have further questions regarding this matter.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Jill Stecher
Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:JS:ak

Enclosure

cc: Mr. Lawrence Augusta
Assistant Chief Counsel
Board of Equalization
Legal Division
P.O. Box 942879
Sacramento, CA 94279-0082
